body:
The Unasked Question

Have you ever wondered what remains when the dream fades? When the frantic chase through impossible corridors ceases, when the ecstatic flight over jeweled cities ends, when the terror of the formless pursuer dissolves with the morning light? We talk so much about our dreams—we analyze their symbols, we dissect their narratives, we mine them for psychological gold. But we rarely ask the most fundamental question: who is the one watching it all? Who is the silent witness to the grand theater of your sleeping mind, and where does that witness go when the show is over? This is not a question for casual contemplation. It is a spiritual drill boring into the bedrock of your perceived reality. The answer, or rather the dissolution of the question itself, leads us to a state of consciousness so profound, so utterly foundational, that it makes the states of waking, dreaming, and even deep, dreamless sleep feel like mere ripples on its surface. This is the state of Turiya, the fourth state. It is the silent, unmoving screen upon which the entire movie of your existence is projected.

Beyond the Trilogy of Consciousness

We are conditioned to believe that our lives are a simple trilogy: we are awake, we are dreaming, or we are in the void of deep sleep. We navigate our waking hours with a sense of solid identity, a "me" that has a name, a history, a list of grievances and desires. In our dreams, this "me" becomes a fluid actor, capable of impossible feats and subject to bizarre transformations, yet the sense of a central experiencer remains. And in deep sleep, we believe it all vanishes into a merciful nothingness, a temporary death from which we are resurrected by the alarm clock. This neat little package is a lie. It is a comfortable, self-limiting story we tell ourselves to avoid the terrifying, liberating truth: that we are infinitely more than the character we play in any of these states. This trilogy is a cage, and most of us live and die without ever realizing we are rattling its bars. We mistake the performance for the performer, the wave for the ocean. Turiya is the ocean itself—the vast, silent, ever-present awareness that is the source and substance of all three states. It is not a state you achieve; it is the state you *are*, stripped of all the noise and costumes. The waking state, with its sharp edges and seemingly solid rules, is a projection of a localized, constricted consciousness. The dreaming state, a realm of fluid symbols and impossible physics, reveals the mind's creative and chaotic power, yet it is still a story being told to a perceived self. The state of deep, dreamless sleep is a plunge into the unmanifest, a temporary dissolution of the storyteller and the story, yet we return from it unchanged, re-emerging into the same familiar patterns. Turiya is the underlying reality that makes all three possible, the canvas that is untouched by the paintings that appear upon it. To know Turiya is to know oneself as this canvas, not as the fleeting images of the waking world or the chaotic dramas of the dream.

Seriously, this existence is just a complicated Flashmob — pretending that it's a forever deal. It's not.

The Divine will roar through you like a wild cyclone from the Beyond — when you stop apologizing for your anger, clarity, needs, voice, and magic.

A woman I know spent three years ignoring this kind of dream before she finally sat down and listened. The message had not changed.

Dick Schwartz's Internal Family Systems model offers a useful lens here: the dream figures are not random characters but parts of your own psyche demanding attention.

The Dream as a Portal, Not a Playground

So much of modern dreamwork is a spiritual cul-de-sac. It becomes another form of entertainment, another way to decorate the walls of our intuitive prison. We learn to lucid dream so we can fly, have sex with movie stars, or conjure magical powers. We interpret symbols to give our ego another fascinating story about itself. This is not the path. This is spiritual tourism. The dream is not a destination; it is a gateway. It is a training ground for the recognition of Turiya. The value of the dream is not in its content, but in its context. It shows you, night after night, that reality is a construct. It demonstrates with vivid, irrefutable evidence that your sense of self is a fabrication, a temporary role. To use the dream for anything less than the radical dismantling of your own identity is to miss the point entirely. It is like being given a key to the universe and using it to open another beer. The dream is a sacred fire that can burn away your illusions, but you have to be willing to step into the flames. You have to be willing to let the dream character you cherish so dearly be consumed, so that the silent witness, the true Self, can finally be known. This requires a radical shift in perspective. Instead of asking, 'What does this dream mean for me?', you must begin to ask, 'What does this dream reveal about the nature of the 'me' who is asking?' Every dream, no matter how mundane or terrifying, is a direct invitation to investigate the structure of your own consciousness. The recurring dream of being unprepared for an exam is not just about anxiety; it's a commentary on the conditioned self that lives in a perpetual state of striving and inadequacy. The dream of flying is not just a wish for freedom; it's a glimpse of the soul's inherent nature, untethered by the laws of the physical world. By turning your attention from the dream's narrative to the dreamer's nature, you transform the dream from a passive experience into an active spiritual practice. You begin to use the dream state to consciously unravel the knots of your own ego, to see through the illusions that bind you in your waking life. This is the true purpose of dreamwork: not to interpret the shadows on the cave wall, but to turn around and face the fire that is casting them.

I ask my higher Self to rise above thought and mind so that I can live and behave in union with the ever-blossoming eternal consciousness.

Confronting the Unseen in the Dreamscape

The dream is where your shadow lives. It is the repository for all the parts of yourself you have disowned, denied, and repressed. The monster chasing you is your own rage. The crumbling house is your own neglected soul. The tidal wave is your own unwept grief. This is why we fear our nightmares, and why we must turn and face them. Someone once sat across from me and said, "I keep dreaming that I m drowning, and I can't find the shore.' That's not just a dream about drowning. That is the soul announcing its suffocation under the weight of a false self. To engage with that dream is not to analyze the water or the shore, but to ask: who is it that believes they are drowning? Who is it that is clinging to a self that is so clearly exhausted and overwhelmed? The work, the real work, is to use the dream's confrontation to see the prison of your own making. It is to see the 'you' that is drowning as a construct, a story, a collection of fears and attachments. And in that seeing, to let it go. To let it drown. Because what is left when the drowning self dissolves is the vast, unsinkable ocean of Turiya.

The mind is just a mutable tool. You are not your mind. You are spirit. Let thoughts bubble away.

'Please respect my needs' to someone who's shown you repeatedly that they won't is not a boundary — it's a prayer to someone who doesn't give a fuck.

The shift continues.

The Witness is Not a Bystander

The path to recognizing Turiya is not passive. It is not about blissfully observing your life from a detached distance. That is another form of spiritual bypassing, a subtle trap of the ego that loves to feel superior and 'above it all.' The witness consciousness is not a neutral observer; it is a fiercely engaged presence. It is the part of you that sees the dream for what it is and chooses not to believe the illusion. It is the part of you that feels the terror of the nightmare but does not identify with the fear. It is the part of you that experiences the joy of the ecstatic dream but does not cling to the pleasure. This is an active, moment-to-moment practice of dis-identification. It is a rebellion against the tyranny of the mind's stories. In your waking life, it means noticing the narratives you spin about yourself and others and consciously refusing to be defined by them. It means feeling your emotions fully without letting them become your identity. In your dream life, it means cultivating the intention to remember that you are dreaming, not to control the dream, but to recognize the dreamer as the dream itself. This is the practice of what some call 'dream yoga,' a profound path that uses the dream state to accelerate the journey to liberation. It is a discipline that requires consistency and a fierce commitment to truth. In the beginning, you will fail. You will forget you are dreaming and be swept away by the narrative. You will identify with the dream character and suffer its imaginary trials. This is not a cause for discouragement. It is part of the process. Each time you remember, even for a fleeting moment, you are strengthening the muscle of awareness. Each time you question the reality of the dream, you are loosening the chains of illusion in your waking life. The practice extends beyond the night. It is about bringing the same quality of questioning awareness to your day. Is this thought true? Is this emotion who I am? Is this reaction coming from a place of freedom or a place of conditioning? The dream is a concentrated, high-stakes version of your waking life. By learning to navigate its illusions, you become more adept at navigating the far more subtle and seductive illusions of the world.

The Wisdom of the Void

Beyond the wild theater of the dreaming mind lies the state of deep, dreamless sleep, or *sushupti*. We tend to dismiss this state as a blank, an absence of experience. We think of it as a nightly reboot, a simple biological function. But the ancient sages of the Upanishads saw it differently. They understood that deep sleep is not an absence, but a presence. It is a temporary return to the undifferentiated source from which all creation arises. In deep sleep, the ego, the 'I' with all its stories and struggles, completely dissolves. There is no dreamer, no dream, no world. There is only pure, objectless consciousness. This is a crucial clue. It shows us that our existence is not dependent on the ego. We can exist, and do exist every night, without it. The problem is that we are not conscious *of* this state. We enter it and leave it in ignorance. The practice, then, is to bring the light of awareness into this darkness. To maintain a thread of witness consciousness even as the mind and the world dissolve. This is an advanced practice, to be sure, but the intention itself is powerful. As the dream researcher Rubin Naiman points out, our modern world is suffering from a profound loss of deep sleep, and with it, a loss of this essential connection to the restorative void. We are over-stimulated, over-caffeinated, and terrified of the dark. We have forgotten how to surrender, how to let go completely. Reclaiming the sanctity of deep sleep is not just about physical health; it is about spiritual sanity. It is about remembering our way back to the source.

You're allowed to burn your 'nice' mask and replace it with nothing — no identity, no role, just the wild holy breath of your actual being.

My consciousness is divine, therefore I am divine.

The Deeper Current of Turiya

Turiya is not a destination you arrive at. It is the ground you are already standing on. It is the silent, ever-present awareness that permeates and transcends the states of waking, dreaming, and deep sleep. It is the light that illuminates the waking world, the dream world, and the void. It is the screen on which the movie of your life plays out. You cannot 'achieve' Turiya because you *are* Turiya. The entire spiritual path is not about becoming something you are not, but about unbecoming everything you have falsely believed yourself to be. It is a process of subtraction, of stripping away the layers of conditioning, the false identities, the borrowed beliefs, until only the essential, irreducible truth of your own being remains. This is not a comfortable process. It is a holy fire. It will burn away your cherished illusions, your comfortable lies, and your carefully constructed sense of self. It will demand that you face the shadow you have been running from your entire life. It will require you to stand naked and unadorned before the mirror of your own consciousness. But the freedom on the other side of that fire is absolute. It is the freedom of the ocean, which is not disturbed by the crashing of the waves on its surface. It is the peace that passes all understanding, because it is not dependent on any external condition. It is the silent, unshakeable joy of being. This is the promise of Turiya. It is the promise of coming home to the truth of who you have always been.

Stop managing how you're perceived. Seriously — stop. You're not here to be edited or consumed. You're here to fully embody your eternal Self.

This journey is the heart of what real shadow work and dream integration is all about. It's not about making your life prettier; it's about making it real. It's about having the courage to die before you die, to let go of the false self so the true Self can live.

Frequently Asked Questions

How can I begin to experience Turiya?

You cannot 'experience' Turiya as an object, because it is the subject, the very awareness that is experiencing. The path is one of subtraction, not addition. Begin by cultivating witness consciousness in your waking life. Notice your thoughts, emotions, and sensations without identifying with them. In your dream life, set a firm intention before sleep to recognize the dream as a dream. This practice, known as dream yoga, weakens the grip of illusion and strengthens your connection to the underlying witness. Don't seek a flashy experience; seek the quiet, unwavering presence that is already here.

Is Turiya the same as enlightenment?

Turiya is the state of enlightenment. It is the recognition and stabilization of your true nature as pure, non-dual awareness. However, the word 'enlightenment' has been co-opted and distorted, often painted as a permanent state of bliss or a superhero-like attainment. The reality is more grounded and far more radical. It is the complete and final disillusionment with the false self, the ego. It is not an escape from the human experience, but a full and fearless engagement with it, free from the distortions of fear and desire. It is the end of seeking, because you have realized there was never anything to find but the seeker itself.

A Tender Unraveling

The path to the fourth state is not a conquest. It is a surrender. It is an unraveling of every knot of identity you have ever tied. It is a tender and brutal homecoming. The mind will fight you. The ego will scream in protest. It will tell you that you are disappearing, that you are dying. And you are. The false 'you' is dying. Let it. Let the stories burn. Let the masks crumble. What remains is the silent, loving, indestructible presence of your own true being. This is the only safety there is. This is the only love that will never leave you.

faqs.question: Can two people share the same dream experience?
faqs.answer: Shared dreaming is reported across cultures and throughout history. Whether you frame it as telepathy, collective unconscious, or coincidence depends on your worldview. What matters is what the shared dream is communicating to each person individually.

faqs.question: Should I be concerned if these dreams are disturbing?
faqs.answer: Disturbing dreams are not the problem — they are the alarm system. The unconscious escalates intensity when gentler messages have been ignored. A disturbing dream is your psyche saying: this matters enough to make you uncomfortable.

faqs.question: How can I work with these dreams practically?
faqs.answer: Start by writing the dream down within five minutes of waking. Do not interpret — describe. Then sit with the images for a day before you try to make meaning. The dream will reveal itself on its own schedule, not yours.
